examples.text: وَمَآ أَكَلَ ٱلسَّبُعُ إِلَّا مَا ذَكَّيْتُمْ وَمَا ذُبِحَ عَلَى ٱلنُّصُبِ
examples.english: You shall not eat that from which a wild animal has eaten, except what you [are able to] ritually slaughter [before its death] and what is slit up on the altars.

etymology.text: In the sense of setting fire causative to ذَكَا (ḏakā, “to flare up”), in the sense of slaughtering probably a loan from Jewish Aramaic, the geminate verb דַּכֵּי (dakkē) usually meaning to “to purify ritually, to clean by separation”, see as well the semantic history of زَكَاة (zakāh).
